serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Kadai Veg is approximately 250 g, served as a main dish alongside naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable curry, mixed vegetables — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for mixed vegetable curry (cooked, oil-based, spiced) used as the primary reference. Kadai veg is a wok-cooked North Indian preparation with onion, capsicum, tomato, and a dry spice blend; macronutrient profile is consistent with ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry data. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect restaurant-level seasoning typical of Singapore North Indian restaurant servings. Values represent a cooked, ready-to-eat dish per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kadai veg curry. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Mixed vegetable curries with oil, tomato, onion, and spice base but no starchy staple typically fall in the GI range 40–55. Serving size 250 g; carbohydrate per serving = 21.25 g; GL = 45 × 21.25 / 100 = 9.6 (rounded to 1 decimal place).

hypertension.guidance: A 250 g restaurant serving provides approximately 950 mg sodium (about 41% of the 2300 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from mixed vegetables
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 45 and 2.4 g dietary fibre per 100 g, kadai veg produces a modest glycaemic load of approximately 9.6 per serving, making it a suitable vegetable dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or plain naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat profile and cholesterol-free vegetable base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kadai veg is cholesterol-free and contains predominantly monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats from vegetable oil, with low saturated fat (0.8 g per 100 g), supporting a heart-healthy dietary pattern when sodium is controlled.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kadai Veg (Spicy) is a North Indian wok-cooked vegetable curry prepared in a traditional kadai (iron wok), a cooking vessel that gives the dish its name. The dish features a colourful mix of vegetables — typically including capsicum (bell pepper), onion, tomato, carrot, and green peas — cooked in a robust dry-spice gravy made from freshly ground kadai masala, which includes coriander seeds, dried red chillies, cumin, and garam masala. The preparation involves tempering whole spices in hot oil, followed by sautéing onion and tomato to form a thick masala base, then tossing the vegetables at high heat to retain texture and absorb the spice blend. The result is a semi-dry, intensely flavoured curry with a bold, smoky, and moderately spicy character. Texturally, the vegetables retain a slight bite while the sauce clings to them rather than forming a thin gravy. In Singapore's North Indian restaurant scene, kadai veg is a popular vegetarian main course served alongside naan, roti, or basmati rice. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (approximately 98 kcal per 100 g) with a favourable macronutrient profile: low saturated fat, meaningful dietary fibre from the vegetable base, and a low glycaemic index of approximately 45. It is suitable for vegetarians and vegans, is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free (in its standard oil-based form), and provides useful micronutrients including vitamin C, vitamin A, iron, and folate from the vegetable components. The primary clinical consideration is sodium, which can be elevated in restaurant preparations.

part of a shared Indian meal
suitable for vegetarian and vegan diners

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for kadai veg curry exists; values are adapted from the closest ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry (cooked, oil-based) entry with sodium adjusted for restaurant context. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the dish composition is well-characterised and the source adaptation is defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry data (cooked, oil-based, spiced) and are representative of a restaurant-prepared kadai veg. Sodium (380 mg per 100 g) is adjusted upward from the ICMR-NIN baseline to reflect typical restaurant seasoning levels observed in Singapore North Indian establishments. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 (confirmed zero — this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients). Vitamin D is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no animal-derived or fortified ingredients are present). Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no partially hydrogenated oils are used in the standard preparation). Cholesterol is 0.0 (confirmed zero — entirely plant-based dish). GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Serving size of 250 g is an estimate based on typical North Indian restaurant portion sizes in Singapore; actual restaurant portions may vary between 200–300 g. Dietary flags assume the standard oil-based preparation without cream or butter; some restaurant versions may include cream or butter, which would affect vegan and dairy-free status.
